coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of organic nanocomposite of polyaniline for enhanced electrochemical performance
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, M. Sathish
Das, Puspendu
Yasoda, K. Yamini
Kothurkar, Nikhil K.
Malik, Sudip
Batabyal, Sudip K.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we have developed a simple and facile technique at 210 °C to obtain nano-structured organic compounds, instead of making carbon dots at a relatively higher temperature. Taking the advantage of stable dispersion of organic nanostructures in an aqueous medium, composite of polyaniline has been developed and subsequently utilized as an active electrode material for supercapacitor application. This composite material confirms noteworthy enhancement in specific capacitance (779 F/g) and also divulges the attractive capacitance retention using three-electrode systems. The suitability of the materials for the solid-state device was tested with a prototype two-electrode device. The symmetric device exhibits 130 F/g gravimetric capacitance and 650 mF/cm2 areal capacitance. The improved performance in the specific capacitance is observed due to the generation of more active charge storage sites in the interfacial interaction of organic nanostructures and polyaniline in the composite. The cyclic stability of the symmetric device reveals excellent retention of 96% in specific capacitance over 5000 cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organic nanostructure
Polyaniline
Supercapacitor
Carbon quantum dots
Photo conducting charge carrier
Cyclic stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assembly of the solid-state symmetric device
Morphological analysis of composites (PANi-ONC-1 and PANi-ONC-2)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), New Delhi, India (Research Grant ECR/2015/000208 ). SM is indebted to CSIR, New Delhi, India (project no. 01(2875)/17/EMR-II) for financial support. First author M. Sathish Kumar is indebted to CSIR New Delhi, India (project no. 09/1034 (0005)/2K19 EMR-I). P. Das is thankful to DST INSPIRE, India for the fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR analysis of sample ON 210 °C (red) and 400 °C (black) (Inset is the structure of IPCA which matches with sample ON 210 °C) (b) TGA analysis of sample ON 210 °C (red) and 400 °C (black) (c) TEM analysis of ON 400 °C (d) TEM analysis of ON 210 °C (Inset is the DLS data of ON 210 °C) (e) Cyclic voltammogram of sample ON 210 °C (red) and 400 °C (black) (f) GCD analysis of ON 210 °C (red) and 400 °C (black). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) FESEM images of PANi-ONC-1 (b) TEM images of PANi-ONC-1 (c) FESEM images of PANi-ONC-2 (d) HR-TEM images of PANi-ONC-2 (Inset figure in PANi-ONC-2 is HR-TEM images of pure 210 °C ON sample).

Electrochemical analysis of (a) Cyclic voltammetry of PANi-ONC-1 (black) and PANi-ONC-2 (red) (A and C represents anodic and cathodic peak) (b) GCD test for PANi-ONC-1 (black) and PANi-ONC-2 (red). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Cyclic voltammetry of the sample PANi-ONC-2 with different scan rates (10–100 mV/s) (A and C represents anodic and cathodic peaks) (b) plot of sample PANi-ONC-2 anodic and cathodic peak currents value from CV image (A1, A2, A3 and C1, C2, C3 corresponds to three different redox peaks) (c) GCD curve of the sample PANi-ONC-2 at different current density (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1 A/g) (d) Ragone plot of the sample PANi, PANi-ONC-1 and PANi-ONC-2 (e) EIS test for the sample PANi, PANi-ONC-1 and PANi-ONC-2 (inset figure is the corresponding circuit of sample PANi-ONC-2) (f) cyclic stability of the sample PANi-ONC-2 at 3 A/g over 3000 cycles.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry of the symmetric device of PANi-ONC-2 with different scan rates (10–100 mV/s) (b) GCD curve of the device of PANi-ONC-2 at different current density (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1 A/g) (c) EIS test for the device along with the fitted curve (inset figure is the corresponding circuit of the device (d) cyclic stability of the device at 1 A/g over 5000 cycles (inset figure in figure 5 (d) is the first ten consecutive GCD cycles and last ten consecutive cycles of two-electrode device long run).
Comparison of various carbon materials decorated with polyaniline and its specific capacitance.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of organic nanocomposite of polyaniline for enhanced electrochemical performance

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dSchool of Applied and Interdisciplinary Sciences, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, 2A and 2B Raja S. C. Mullick Road, Jadavpur, Kolkata 700032, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we have developed a simple and facile technique at 210 °C to obtain nano-structured organic compounds, instead of making carbon dots at a relatively higher temperature. Taking the advantage of stable dispersion of organic nanostructures in an aqueous medium, composite of polyaniline has been developed and subsequently utilized as an active electrode material for supercapacitor application. This composite material confirms noteworthy enhancement in specific capacitance (779 F/g) and also divulges the attractive capacitance retention using three-electrode systems. The suitability of the materials for the solid-state device was tested with a prototype two-electrode device. The symmetric device exhibits 130 F/g gravimetric capacitance and 650 mF/cm2 areal capacitance. The improved performance in the specific capacitance is observed due to the generation of more active charge storage sites in the interfacial interaction of organic nanostructures and polyaniline in the composite. The cyclic stability of the symmetric device reveals excellent retention of 96% in specific capacitance over 5000 cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic nanostructures (ON) comprising different molecules recently intrigues huge research interest [1] for the reason that they have tunable optical, electrical and optoelectrical properties and diversified applications in different fields like light-emitting devices [2], multichannel optical routers [3], photo-switches [4], photo-detectors [5], electrochemistry [6], and energy devices [7]. Even though inorganic nanodots offer good and tunable optoelectronic behavior [8], there is a question in toxicity that hinders the usage in many applications and still uncertain in their scalability [9]. The understanding of the elementary rules that regulate emission in carbon nanodots (CDs) in connection to their structure will create advancement in permitting fluorescent carbon to compete for the inorganic semiconductor quantum dots [6]. Currently, organic hetero nanocomposites are considered to be a promising material for various application and molecular design because of their simple processability, mechanical flexibility, tendency to self-assemble owing to Van der walls forces, dipole-dipole interactions and π-π stacking. The common method by fractionation/fragmentation of organic carbon sources succeeded by surface passivation is used to obtain CDs [10-12]. The presence of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in the core of CDs was found by Fu et al. [13]. Sarkar et al. probed the theoretical investigation of nitrogen-functionalized CDs, connecting their absorption properties with the presence of different nitrogen coordination sites within the CDs framework [14]. Both top-down and bottom-up approaches can be used for the synthesis of CDs [7, 15]. The top-down procedure is used to fragment the bulk carbon material to nanostructures by physical or chemical approaches [16-18]. In bottom-up synthesis, complex chemical reactions are conducted at high pressure and temperature, in several steps, generally called polymerization and carbonization [19]. In recent days, CDs are synthesized using one of the most often used bio-product citric acid (CA) due to the direct process and high fluorescent quantum yields (QYs) [15, 20-26]. Amongst all the previous reports the fluorescent CDs prepared from citric acid (CA) and amines via heating, hydrothermal method or microwave-assisted technique have been preferred as best methods due to relatively high quantum yield (QY). Recently, to study the mechanism of fluorescence of CDs due to the formation of fluorescent citrazinic acid derivative was reported by the song's group [27]. Kasprzyk et al. conferred the formation of this class of organic fluorophores from the reaction of citric acid with α, β-diamines, or similar molecules with double functionality (alcohol, thiol) like cystine maintained at 180 °C for 1 h [12, 28]. Using the hydrothermal method, Schneider et al. stated the potential impact of molecular fluorophores on the optical property of three different species of CDs, based on citric acid and three nitrogen precursors [29]. Krysmann et al. investigated C-dots using citric acid and ethanolamine precursors at different temperatures [30]. Shoujun Zhu et al. synthesized photo luminescent CDs with citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors through the hydrothermal method and explored CDs properties [17]. A big question of discussion is open to understanding the organic nanostructures in the carbon quantum dots (CDs) with its undetermined chemical structure and origin of their fluorescence nature [31-34]. Therefore, identifying the compositions and determining the related chemical structure is essential for resolving the fluorescence origin of citric acid derived carbon dots. Recently, the possible origin of their luminescent properties has been established by isolation and identification of luminescent species in a citric acid derivative group. A brief analysis expounds on the chemical structure of citricacid-amine derived organic molecules or fluorophores [28, 35, 36]. The high-intensity emission probably originates from molecular species: i.e. 1,2,3,5-tetrahydro-5-oxo-imidazo (1,2-a) pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (IPCA), in case of CD synthesis using CA and ethylenediamine, EDA) [37, 6]. All the groups as mentioned earlier of the previous report reveal that the PL intensity and quantum yields of the CDs from CA-amine complex becomes very low as the pyrolysis is subjected to high temperature because the high intense molecular fluorophore is gradually consumed as the building block of lesser photoluminescent carbogenic core [38, 39]. Therefore, the low-temperature pyrolysis process gives the impression as the major key to preserving the fluorescence behavior. Though there are few reports on the formation of organic nanostructure during CDs synthesis in the hydrothermal method. To best of our knowledge, the study on the organic nanostructures at a less time-consuming low temperature (direct heating method) process has not yet been established. In this work, we have synthesized organic nanostructures (ON) using citric acid and ethylenediamine as the precursors by direct heating for a short duration instead of previously reported hydrothermal or microwave-based method. The obtained product may promote more interactive sites for charge storage when incorporated with polyaniline. Polyaniline (PANi) is a superior material for supercapacitor because of its low-cost synthesis, good processability, and multi-redox property [40-42], but the difficulty of PANi is that it suffers from poor cyclic stability and mechanical stability [43, 44]. We have used this organic nanostructure for the first time during the polymerization of aniline to improve its specific capacitance as well as the cyclic stability for supercapacitor application.

Citric acid (C6H8O7, 99%), ethylenediamine (C2H4(NH2)2), (99%), distilled aniline (C6H5NH2, 99%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 35–38%), ammonium per oxi-di sulfide (APS) ((NH4)2S2O8, 98%), sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), potassium hydroxide (KOH, 85%), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, 98%), N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 99.3%), potassium bromide (KBr, 99.9%), polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), activated carbon (grade 900 IV), Isopropyl alcohol (IPA) (CH3CH(OH)CH3, 99%), Nafion d-521 dispersions, Acetone ((CH3)2CO, 99%), Alumina (Al2O3, 99%), carbon cloth (flip India) and double distilled water were used for the synthesis.

The organic nanostructures and carbon dots were prepared using citric acid-EDA complex at both low and high-temperature conditions. For the synthesis of organic nanostructures, initially, 3.00 g of citric acid was taken along with 10 ml of ethylenediamine after 15 min sonication and it resulted in a white color gel-like viscous product. After annealing the gel-like viscous product at 210 °C for 1 h, a brownish-yellow color product was obtained as shown in SI1 (a) (Supporting Information), and it was taken for further characterizations. For comparison, the same experimental procedure was followed to synthesize carbon dots at 400 °C for 1 h. Based on the temperature variation, at 210 °C and 400 °C, organic nanostructures and carbon dots were formed as shown in SI1 (a).

The as-synthesized ON (0.05 g) was taken and mixed with distilled aniline (4.7 g) alongside 10 ml of 1 M HCl. The precursor solution was sonicated for 30 min to get a homogeneous mixture. Later 50 ml of oxidizing agent APS (11.409 g) was added to it and was stirred vigorously and was refrigerated for 12 h for polymerization. The appearance of green color was an indication of PANi formation. The green color product was centrifuged and washed several times with the distilled water and methanol to remove the unreacted oligomers and was dried at 60 °C in the hot air oven overnight. The final synthesized product was named as PANi-ONC-1 (400 °C) and PANi-ONC-2 (210 °C). For evaluation purposes, pure polyaniline was also prepared by the chemical oxidative method without any organic or carbon quantum dots. All the synthesized materials were analyzed thoroughly.

The structure of PANi-ONC-1 and PANi-ONC-2 nanocomposites that were obtained from FT-IR spectra, recorded using the Thermo Scientific Nicolet IS10 IR spectrometer in the midrange (400–4000 cm−1). Using a hydraulic press, pellets were made with a 1:1 ratio of the pristine sample and the KBr. UV–visible spectra were obtained using Shimadzu UV–vis 1800 spectrophotometer for liquid samples. Photoluminescence studies were performed on a Shimadzu RF-6000 instrument. The morphology of the nanocomposites was characterized using Carl Zeiss ultra 55, field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken in an FEI TECNAI G2 S-Twin microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The thermal stability of the sample was characterized using TA instruments [SDT Q600]. CHN analyzes were conducted using 2400 SERIES II CHNS/ O ANALYZER (Perkin Elmer).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique were carried out in an electrochemical workstation (ZIVE sp1, WonAtech Co., Ltd) using the three-electrode system. Before the measurements, polishing of the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (3 mm diameter, surface area 0.07 cm2) was done using 0.05 mm alumina suspension and washed with double-distilled water and finally with acetone. The homogeneous slurry was made by adding the double-distilled water, isopropyl alcohol, and nafion with active materials. The working electrode was prepared from the synthesized samples by making a slurry. The slurry was deposited carefully upon GCE and dried under an IR lamp for 15 min. The mass of the active material on the electrode surface was 0.098 mg. The three-electrode measurement was carried out using a cell equipped with glassy carbon as a working electrode, Ag/AgCl as a reference, and Pt (platinum) wire as a counter electrode in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of the solid-state symmetric device
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques were carried out in an electrochemical workstation (ZIVE sp1, WonAtech Co., Ltd) using the two-electrode solid-state device. The symmetric supercapacitor device (SSCs) was assembled by utilizing PANi-ONC-2 as the positive and negative electrodes with poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA)/H2SO4 gel electrolyte. The PVA/H2SO4 gel electrolyte was prepared by mixing 3 g PVA in 30 ml of distilled water with vigorous stirring at 85 °C until a clear homogeneous solution was formed. Then 3 g of H2SO4 added to the mixture solution and allowed to cool down at room temperature to form a gel. Before fabrication of the solid-state two-electrode symmetric device, the substrate carbon clothes were soaked in each solution with acetone, 1 M H2SO4, and DI water for two hours respectively, to eliminate impurities. The cleaned substrates were then soaked overnight to improve hydrophilicity in the 'Piranha solution' (concentrated H2SO4 and 30% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in the volume ratio of 3:1). At last, the functionalized substrates were kept in milli-Q water to prevent the recovery of hydrophobicity. To fabricate the symmetric two-electrode device, 50 mg of active material (PANi-ONC-2) taken together with distilled water (250 μl), IPA (isopropyl alcohol) (250 μl) and Nafion (10 μl) were added and sonicated for half an hour to form a slurry. From this slurry, 50 microliters were taken and coated on the carbon cloth with a geometrical area of approximately 1 × 1 cm2 and dried at 70 °C. After that, the gel electrolyte was applied to both the electrodes and firmly sandwiched to prevent damages or any leakages. The total mass of the active material coated on the carbon cloth substrate is 5 mg. Thus, we have successfully assembled a solid-state symmetric device, which is also explained in the schematic representation of the device fabrication SI2.

FT-IR analysis divulges the presence of functional groups from the synthesized nanostructures, which help to understand the final product of sample ON 210 °C (color-coded red in Fig. 1 a) and ON 400 °C (color-coded black in Fig. 1a). The stretching vibration was between 3100 and 3500 cm−1 is attributed to N-H/O-H stretching, the vibration fingerprints at 1555 cm−1 correspond to CC stretching of the citric acid carbon core, the peaks at 2060 cm−1 corresponds to CO group from carbonyl functionalities and the strong peak at 1715 cm−1 associated with the CO, which indicates the presence of α, β unsaturated carboxylic acid moieties and derivatives on the oxidized edges, which can be seen in citrazinic acid derivatives (IPCA) in the ON sample [6, 29]. This peak in ON 210 °C is comparatively more intense than that in ON 400 °C, clearly indicating that the obtained fluorophore in the low-temperature process contains only organic moieties (IPCA). These peaks are diminished in the composite specifying that the peaks are utilized for the formation of PANi composites. Additional peaks at 1635 cm−1, 1400 cm−1 corresponds to the CH bond vibration and CH3 deformations. Several bands observed in the range 1130–1250 cm−1 in the sample ON 210 °C are caused by mixed IR absorbance of -O- and CN bonds of material defects in the molecular fluorophores. The peak observed at 584 cm−1 belongs to the NH vibration mode which is from the amine group. Figure SI3 shows the FT-IR spectra of synthesized PANi-ONC-1 and PANi-ONC-2 composite. The stretching vibrations between 3100 and 3500 cm−1 correspond to O-H and N-H bonds. The peaks at 2097 cm−1 are associated with CO bonds. The peaks lying between 1380 and 1500 cm−1 and around 800 cm−1 corresponds to CH stretching. The CC bonds from benzenoid and quinoid rings of PANi peaks are revealed at 1590 cm−1. The surface hydroxylation O-H stretching vibrations of PANi composites is found at 1308 cm−1. N-H stretching vibrations from PANi is observed at 621 cm−1. All these distinctive vibrations indicate the presence of functional groups of ON and PANi composites. Figure SI1 (b) shows the UV–visible spectroscopy for the sample 210 °C ON (color-coded red in the figure) and 400 °C ON (color-coded black in the figure). Two peaks were distinctly seen in both samples. The peak at 260 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition state which belongs to the CC bond from aromatic rings. The peak at 420 nm in 400 °C ON and the high intense peak at 360 nm in 210 °C ON belongs to the n-π* transition states, this characteristic shift and intense peak observed in the 210 °C ON sample is accounted to the presence of the organic moieties. Figure SI1 (c) shows the fluorescence image of sample 210 °C and 400 °C ON under visible and UV irradiation at 365 nm. Both the samples have photoemissive properties, but comparatively high intense turquoise color emission observed for the sample 210 °C is attributed to the aromatic ring formation from the mixture of organic moieties, whereas light green emission is observed for the sample 400 °C ON. These properties are further confirmed by the PL process which is shown in Fig. SI1 (d). The emission peak at 457 nm in the sample 210 °C ON and 467 nm in the sample 400 °C ON are observed when excited the wavelength at 410 nm. The maximum emission is observed in sample 210 °C ON in this particular range because of the organic moieties (IPCA) either embedded within the sp3 carbon core (CDs) or attached to its surface [6]. Yang and co-workers established that the formation of IPCA (citrazinic acid derivative) is the major reason for the optical properties of citric acid-amine based CDs [21, 27]. Righetto et al. showed that the maximum intensity observed in the excitation range between 350 and 450 nm is due to the presence of free photoluminescent molecules in solution, which is responsible for this high emissive property. Whereas, the excitation wavelength exceedingly above 480 nm allows for the observation of only selected carbon core [6]. This is evident that the emission from sample 210 °C ON due to consists of a photoluminescent molecule named IPCA (organic citrazinic moieties) and sample 400 °C ON consists of almost only carbon dots. Fig. 1(b) shows the TGA curve of ON synthesized samples at 210 °C (red) and 400 °C (black). In the sample 210 °C ON the second decomposition is attributed to organic nanostructures, whereas this decomposition was not perceived in the sample synthesized at 400 °C. It reveals that the product synthesized at higher temperature consists of only C-dots. The supporting evidence vouches that in low temperature synthesized product (210 °C) organic nanostructure mixture is found [45]. Above 400 °C, the decomposition is attributed to only carbon in all samples. Figure SI4 shows the TGA curve of PANi-ONC-1 and PANi-ONC-2 composite. The initial decomposition is credited to adsorbed moisture in both samples. In the PANi-ONC composites, the second decomposition is associated with the dopant in the sample. The third decomposition fits into the polymer backbone degradation of PANi composites. The final decomposition of the composite belongs to the chain length reduction of the polymer. The HRTEM image displayed in Fig. 1(c) shows that the sample 400 °C ON contains around 1–3 nm of quantum dots. The HRTEM image of 400 °C ON sample shows fringes indexed having 3.6 Å interplanar distance [10] is shown in Fig. SI5 (a), The corresponding FFT (fast Fourier transform) image seen in Figure SI5 (b). These results clearly reveal that the synthesized 400 °C ON samples consist of small carbon dots with core graphitic structure. This facile method can be considered as a large scale simple and cost-effective method for fabrication of graphitic nontoxic CQDs powder with a dimension of 1–3 nm size for various applications. TEM studies clearly show the spherical morphologies of around 50–500 nm displayed in Fig. 1(d). The inset shows DLS data of the 210 °C ON sample with a size range between 50 and 500 nm, which clearly ties with the TEM data. For a clear understanding, few other TEM images of 210 °C ON showed in Figure SI5 (c) and (d). Further confirmations also are done by using the CHN analyzer and found nearly 6% hydrogen for the sample 210 °C ON and only 3% hydrogen found for sample 400 °C ON. This data also proves that sample 210 °C ON has a mixture of organic nanostructure SI6. Cyclic voltammogram of the sample ON 210 °C and 400 °C shown in Fig. 1(e). In the sample, 210 °C ON can be seen redox peaks, and as well as enhanced area proved the presence of organic mixtures in the sample. Whereas in the sample 400 °C ON shows almost double-layer behavior and small enhancement in an area clearly indicates, the high temperature synthesized sample contains almost carbon only. The high enhancement can be seen for the sample 210 °C ON compared to the sample 400 °C ON in GCD analysis shown in Fig. 1(f). These above findings are well established that the sample 210 °C ON is a superior and suitable material for making PANi nanocomposite than 400 °C ON sample in the field of a supercapacitor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological analysis of composites (PANi-ONC-1 and PANi-ONC-2)
ce:section.ce:para: Morphologies of PANi-ONC (1 & 2) composites were investigated by electron microscopy analysis. In sample PANi-ONC-1 composite interconnected rod-like structures are observed in SEM (Fig. 2 a). The high-resolution TEM image clearly shows dark spots of carbon dots decorated on PANi rods in Fig. 2b. The PANi-ONC-2 composite material showed a large number of agglomerated spherical nanostructures form interconnected plaited PANi chains as identified in SEM (Fig. 2c). In Fig. 2d, a dark contrast HRTEM image shows spherical nanoparticles sized around 50–500 nm (circled in the yellow ring) are seen along with the PANi rods. This may the close interactions between organic nanostructure and PANi, which leads to better electrochemical behavior. Some more representatives of PANi-ONC-1 and PANi-ONC-2 HRTEM images at different scales are given in Figure SI7.

Two different PANi composites were synthesized using two different ON in the same environment and its electrochemical performances were investigated. The cyclic voltammetry analysis was done using Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode, a platinum wire as a counter electrode, and the synthesized samples were coated on the working electrode (glassy carbon electrode with 3 mm diameter) in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte solution. The performance of the sample carried out at 100 mV/s scan rate with the potential range between −0.2 to 0.8 V. Typically, PANi shows multi redox behavior in this particular range, as leucoemeraldine (fully reduced state), emeraldine (partially reduced and oxidized state) and pernigraniline (fully oxidized state) during the electrochemical investigation, these three pairs of redox peaks appearance are due to the faradaic reaction occurring in polyaniline chain. In Fig. 3 (a) the peaks A1/C1 belongs to the transition from leucoemeraldine to emeraldine, the small signature in the peaks A2/C2 is related to the conversion of benzenoid to quinoid, the peaks A3/C3 belongs to emeraldine to pernigraniline [40, 46, 47] (A and C represents anodic and cathodic peaks) indicating the presence of PANi. The carbon materials (ON structures) exhibiting EDLC behaviors, maybe hidden behind these peaks. Both the materials show good electrochemical behavior, relatively the enhancement in the area, and current was observed in sample PANi-ONC-2. Galvanostatic charge and discharge analysis were done in the same environment with a current density of 0.2 A/g. The discharge time is fairly increased in PANi-ONC-2 sample when compared to the sample PANi-ONC-1, due to the strong interfacial interaction between PANi and organic nanostructure. Since the sample ON 210 °C consists of aromatic ring structures, which has hexagon π-π interaction with polyaniline causing better electrochemical performances. This result clearly indicates that the organic nanostructure (ON 210 °C) backs in improving the electron transport path. As a result, we have focused on further studies on PANi-ONC-2.

Fig. 4 (a) represents the cyclic voltammetry of the PANi-ONC-2 at different scan rates within the range of (10–100 mV/s). At all scan rates, the shape of redox peaks in the cyclic voltammetry of PANi-ONC-2 is well retained. The appearance of three pairs of redox peaks corresponds to the different transition states of PANi as discoursed in the previous passage. The asymmetric nature of the CV curves implies the contribution of the double layer from organic nanostructure and pseudocapacitive nature from PANi of the composites. The linear increase in the three sets of redox peaks (A1, A2, A3 and C1, C2, C3) of PANi-ONC-2 (Fig. 4(b)) are in accordance with the increasing scan rates proving the low internal resistance, good reversible stability, and fast redox process. Fig. 4(c), GCD curves of PANi-ONC-2 at different current density (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1 A/g) shows the quasi-triangular shape of the curve indicating the presence of both double-layer as well as pseudo-capacitive behavior in the composite. The calculated specific capacitance value of PANi-ONC-2 is 779 F/g at 0.2 A/g, this substantial enhancement is the contribution of the double-layer capacitance of ON and faradaic capacitance of PANi. The previous reported specific capacitance of PANi with other carbon materials are given in Table (1) , which clearly indicates the better electrochemical performance achieved by this organic nanostructure. The GCD result with different current densities of only PANi and PANi-ONC-1 are shown in Figure SI8 (a) and SI8 (b). The comparison cyclic voltammetry of pure PANi and PANi-ONC-2 at 100 mV/s are shown in the Figure SI8 (c) and comparison GCD profile of PANi and PANi-ONC-2 at 0.2 A/g are shown in Figure SI8 (d). The calculated specific capacitance of the synthesized samples at different current densities are shown in Figure SI8 (e). These results clearly show the significant enhancement in the sample PANi-ONC-2, as compared to pure PANi the strong interaction of organic nanostructure with PANi leads to improvement of the electrochemical charge storage efficiency in the PANi chain. Fig. 4(d) shows the Ragone plot of power density vs. energy density curve for PANi, PANi-ONC-1, and PANi-ONC-2 samples. The PANi-ONC-2 sample achieves an increased energy density of 37 Wh/kg at a power density of 322 W/kg. This is a much better improvement compared to PANi-ONC-1 and PANi, which implies the major role of organic nanostructure decorated in PANi. The electrochemical impedance analysis of the sample PANi, PANi-ONC-1, and PANi-ONC-2 are shown in Fig. 4(e). EIS was plotted by taking an imaginary part in X-axis and real part in Y-axis. The Nyquist plot of the samples PANi-ONC-1 and PANi-ONC-2 exhibiting small semicircle response in the high-frequency region compared to pure PANi. These results indicate the low charge transfer resistance due to the addition of nanomaterials (organic nanostructure and CQDs as well). This behavior possibly gives efficient ion diffusion property to the sample at an electrode and electrolyte surface. The upward lines of the samples in the low-frequency region indicate the capacitive behavior of the sample. The inset in Fig. 4(e) is the corresponding EIS circuit image of the sample PANi-ONC-2. The cyclic stability of the sample PANi-ONC-2 (Fig. 4(f)) shows the 86.3% retention of specific capacitance at a current density of 3 A/g over 3000 cycles, whereas neat PANi has 80% retention for only 500 cycles at a current density of 3 A/g shown in Figure SI8 (f). These results infer the major contribution of organic nanostructure and help to enhance the electrochemical performance of PANi.

The suitability of the synthesized materials was investigated for a prototype two-electrode symmetric device. The electrochemical performance was analysed for the carbon cloth-based solid-state device shown in Fig. 5 . Cyclic voltammetry of the symmetric device was performed at different scan rate in the range of 10 mV/s to 100 mV/s. The enhancement in the area under the curve for cyclic voltammetry of the PANi-ONC-2 device indicates the excellent capacitive role of the device. Fig. 5(b) show the GCD profile of symmetric device with different current density (0.2 A/g to 1 A/g concerning the mass of the coated material). The quasi triangular peaks observed at various current densities without any changes in the GCD profile indicates the better reversibility of materials. The calculated specific capacitance of the device from the GCD tests was 130, 105.6, 95.6, 84, and 73 F/g at 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1 A/g current densities respectively, that shown in the Figure SI9 (a) as specific capacitance (F/g) vs. current density (A/g). The corresponding calculated energy density and the power density were arrived using a Ragone plot of the device also shown in Figure SI9 (b). This device can successfully produce 11.6 Wh/kg energy density at 80.3 W/kg power density. The areal capacitance of the device (i.e. 650, 528.3, 476.8, 420 and 364.4 mF/cm2) was calculated with different current densities (1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 mA/cm2), concerning only the active material coated in the particular geometrical area (1 × 1 cm2) and shown in the Figure SI9 (c) specific capacitance (mF/cm2) vs. current density (mA/cm2). The Ragone plot of the areal device produced 0.06 mWh/cm2 energy density at 0.4 mW/cm2 power density as shown in Figure SI9 (d). The electrochemical impedance analysis was done for the device PANi-ONC-2. The Nyquist plot of the device shows a small semicirculer responsive behavior in the high-frequency region which corresponds to the low charge transfer resistance. The almost straight upward line in the low-frequency region implies the capacitive behavior of the device shown in Fig. 5(c). The inset in Fig. 5(c) corresponds to the equivalent circuit image of the fabricated symmetric device. The cyclic stability of the device is shown in Fig. 5(d) there is excellent retention in specific capacitance of about 96% over 5000 cycles at a current density of 1 A/g. The inset figure in Fig. 5(d) shows the first and last ten consecutive GCD cycles. The GCD profile shape of the last ten cycles is matched with the initial ten cycles showing that the capability and the reversibility of the device have been proven without damage or harm to the stability of the device. This high retention of the PANi-ONC-2 based symmetric device proves the utilization of the long term application without loss or irregularity. All these analyses conclude that the effective response of the organic nanostructure decorated PANi (PANi-ONC-2) material can be considered as a striking material for the supercapacitor electrode.

In this present study, organic nanostructures and carbon quantum dots were synthesized by simple direct heating at low temperatures and high temperature respectively. The synthesized product was characterized by optical investigations and electron microscope analysis. The synthesized organic nanostructures were used to make the PANi composites by taking advantage of stable dispersion and its electrochemical performance exhibited high specific capacitance for PANi-ONC-2. The obtained composites were characterized using FT-IR and UV–vis analysis to know the functional bonds. The morphological studies (SEM and TEM) revealed the decoration of organic nanostructures on the PANi matrix. The higher specific capacitance (779 F/g) and good cyclic stability (86.3%) found in sample PANi-ONC-2 due to the presence of more active charge storage sites and higher interfacial interaction between organic nanostructure and PANi. The gravimetric capacitance of 130 F/g and the areal capacitance of 650 mF/cm2 were achieved by the two-electrode symmetric device. The excellent cyclic stability of the symmetric device with 96% retention in specific capacitance was obtained. Thus, the unique organic nanostructure decorated polyaniline composite can be considered to be a promising active electrode material for supercapacitors.

M. Sathish Kumar: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing - original draft. Puspendu Das: Formal analysis. K. Yamini Yasoda: Methodology. Nikhil K. Kothurkar: Supervision. Sudip Malik: Supervision. Sudip K. Batabyal: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), New Delhi, India (Research Grant ECR/2015/000208). SM is indebted to CSIR, New Delhi, India (project no. 01(2875)/17/EMR-II) for financial support. First author M. Sathish Kumar is indebted to CSIR New Delhi, India (project no. 09/1034 (0005)/2K19 EMR-I). P. Das is thankful to DST INSPIRE, India for the fellowship.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.est.2020.101700.
